preamble: The shift from the lateral approximant [l] to the alveolar stop [d] is a form of consonant hardening (fortition). It is less common than the reverse lenition but occurs in specific phonological environments such as gemination (doubling) or through pre-stopping, where a stop is inserted before the lateral.

examples.note: Historical geminate LL hardened into a pre-stopped lateral or stop-lateral cluster.
The Latin geminate lateral hardened into an alveolar or retroflex stop.
